Laboratory Practical
1


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Part 2 (can be completed during same lab period or in 2 hour time 
block later)
Identification of the Unknown
•Melting Point
•Empirical Formula
•IR Spectrum
•1H NMR spectrum
•13C NMR spectrum
Overview
Part 1 (during 5 hour lab period):
(1) Extraction – Isolation of the desired unknown from a binary mixture.
(2) Recrystallization – Purification of the desired unknown.
(3) Melting Point – Determination of purity.
2


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Rules of Engagement
Part 1: Isolation, Purification, and Melting Point of Unknown
• Bring only a writing implement. You will be given blank paper at the 
beginning of the laboratory period. 
• Do not bring your textbook or laboratory notebook.
• Specific procedural questions should be addressed to the instructor.
• Turn in your written work at the end of the laboratory period (5 hours).
Part 2: Spectroscopic Identification of Unknown
• You will be given your written work from and additional blank paper. You 
may also bring pages 16 - 27 of the lab practical notes.
• After reporting the melting point, the instructor will give you a packet of 
data that has the empirical formula, IR spectrum and 1H and 13C NMR 
spectra of the unknown.
• From the empirical formula and melting point, preliminarily identify your 
unknown by a literature search (e.g., Aldrich catalog). Based on the 
melting point and spectroscopic data, defend the identification of your 
unknown.
• Turn in your full report.
3

*Further dry the ether layer with anhydrous sodium sulfate, then 
decant the solution into a round bottom flask and evaporate the 
ether using the rotary evaporator. 
In general, semi-wet compounds can be dried by dissolving them 
in 15 mL of ether, adding anhydrous sodium sulfate, decanting 
the dried solution into a round-bottom flask, and then evaporating

Separation of a Binary Mixture
7


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Recrystallization
Step 1: Choose the solvent.
• Rule of Thumb: Like dissolves like
• Single solvent system
-Dissolve solid in hot solvent and then cool
• Mixed solvent system
-Select a solvent pair that is miscible
-Dissolve solid in "good" solvent and heat to boil. Add "bad" solvent 
until solution becomes cloudy and then add "good" solvent until 
solution just clarifies. Allow solution to cool
Step 2: Dissolve the solute. 
• Rule of thumb: Minimum amount of solvent.
• Cover the solute with solvent and heat the mixture. Slowly add additional 
hot solvent until the solute just dissolves.
• If the solute amount is less than 0.2 grams, carry out the recrystallization 
in a test tube.
8

=== PAGE BREAK ===

Step 3: Remove the insoluble and colored impurities (if present). 
• Use Norit to remove colored impurities.
• Use hot gravity filtration to remove solid impurities. Add 10% excess 
solvent before filtering. Evaporate excess solvent after filtration.
Step 4: Crystallize and collect.
• Slow cooling affords the best crystals.
• Collect solid using Büchner funnel.
• If there is too little solid to collect using a Büchner funnel, 
carefully draw off the solvent using a pipette.
Recrystallization
12


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Melting Point Determination
Experimental Technique: 
(1) Crush the dry sample to a fine powder
(2) Fill the capillary with a column of solid less than 2 mm in height.
Pack the solid tightly.
(3) Heat to within about 20°C of the melting point and then decrease 
the rate of heating to less than 2°C/minute.
(4) Record the melting range (from the first drop of liquid until the
solid disappears). Melting range is ~2°C for pure samples.
Impure samples: 
(1) Broaden melting point range (>2°C)
(2) Depressed melting point
13

Preliminary Identification
• Empirical Formula implies the possibilities:
CnHmOx, C2nH2mO2x, C3nH3mO3x…
• Look up possible compounds in catalog and list all compounds within a 
5ºC melting point range.
• Possible compounds:
-Unknown carboxylic acid implies
-Unknown neutral implies no 
-Unknown phenol implies

15


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Spectroscopic Analysis
IR Spectroscopy
• Band Location – Functional group identification
Proton-Decoupled 13C NMR Spectroscopy
• Chemical Shift - Functional group identification
• 13C Signals – Chemically inequivalent carbons are chemical shift 
inequivalent
1H NMR Spectroscopy
• Chemical Shift - Functional group identification
• Integration – Relative number of hydrogens responsible for each signal
• Multiplicity – Signal splitting gives the number of proton neighbors
-The neighbors are typically within three bonds
-N + 1 Rule: N equivalent neighbors split a signal into N +1 lines
-Pascal’s Triangle: The multiplet line intensities are given by the 
possible combinations of the a and b spins states of the neighbors
-Coupling Constants: The magnitude of the coupling constant 
determines the geometric relationship of proton neighbors
16

=== PAGE BREAK ===

Interpretation Hints
Melting Point and Empirical Formula 
• Generate a list of possible compounds from the available catalogs.
• Initially list all compounds within a ±5C range of the observed m.p. 
• Consider only the compounds that have the desired functional group.
IR spectral clues
• The frequency axis is often not linear. Depending on the scale, peaks 
may appear more narrow or wider than expected.
• C=C double bond stretches are often weak and may not be discernable 
in the IR spectrum.
• The frequency of a carbonyl stretch is decreased by conjugation with 
double bonds:
R
O
R

R
O
1700 - 1665 cm-1
1700 - 1680 cm-1 
24


=== PAGE BREAK ===

Proton-decoupled 13C NMR spectral clues
• The number of 13C signals may not exceed the number of carbons in 
the compound.
• Fewer signals than expected may imply that symmetry is present. 
However, carbons not bonded to hydrogens also may not give 
observable 13C signals.
• Ignore signal intensity since it is not indicative of the number of carbons 
present.
• The 13C NMR signals due to the solvent are labeled with a s.
• Use the chemical shift to identify possible functional groups.

=== PAGE BREAK ===

1H NMR spectral clues
• Chemical Shift: 
–Conjugation with a carbonyl shifts hydrogens further downfield than 
expected:
–Phenolic hydrogens (Ar-OH) are typically found in the 5 - 10 ppm
region.
–Since the chemical shift of carboxylic acid protons is found in the 
11 - 13 ppm region and the range of the given 1H NMR spectrum is 
from -0.5 to 10 ppm, the signal of the carboxylic acid proton is not 
shown or is given as an offset. Add the offset value to 10 ppm to 
obtain the chemical shift value.
• Integration: 
–Bring a ruler

Interpretation Hints
26


=== PAGE BREAK ===

1H NMR spectral clues
• Multiplicity: 
–Splitting patterns may be the only method for distinguishing between 
two or more possible isomers for the unknown (other than the mixed 
melting point).
–Exchanging hydrogens often give broad signals and do not spin-spin 
split their neighbors.
–The splitting of some multiplets may be too complex to interpret. This 
is particularly true for cyclic compounds.
–Methylene hydrogens adjacent to an asymmetric atom are chemically 
different and often have different chemical shifts. These methylene
hydrogens will spin-spin split each other.
–Proton signals may overlap.
–The 1H NMR signals due to the solvent are labeled with a s.
Interpretation Hints
